coredata.dc:title: Physics based modelling of porous lithium ion battery electrodes—A review
coredata.prism:issueName: 4th Annual CDT Conference in Energy Storage & Its Applications
coredata.dc:creator: Le Houx, James
Kramer, Denis
coredata.dc:description: Mathematical models have been used extensively to simulate physical and electrochemical processes occurring inside lithium-ion batteries. Physical based models, coupled with experimental validation, have revealed greater scientific understanding of the processes inside the battery. A region of specific interest is the porous electrode. However, the heterogeneous geometry of the porous structure presents practical difficulties in developing suitable models. The present paper is a review of the studies on the physical modelling of lithium ion porous electrodes. Here we review common methods to model the (de)intercalation behaviour of porous Li-ion battery electrodes. Advantages and drawbacks are contrasted to highlight some challenges that suggest directions and priorities for further research in the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Review
Li-ion battery
Image-based modelling
Porous electrode

UK Wind Energy Research - Centre for Doctoral Training

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the EPSRC Centre for Doctoral Training (CDT), United Kingdom in Energy Storage and its Applications [grant ref: EP/R021295/1 ]. The authors would also like to thank Ewan Fraser for his enlightening discussion and useful comments on this topic.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison of 8 methods to calculate tortuosity factor for the solid oxide cathode sample reported in [30], the presented model is calculated using TauFactor [29].

(A) A heterogeneous microstructure,
Ω
0
, featuring three phases:
Ω
AP
active particle (yellow),
Ω
E
electrolyte (green) and
Ω
B
conductive binder (grey), each with their own transport properties. (B) The homogenised equivalent domain, with transport properties of the three phases represented as effective coefficients. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Main features of the physics based porous electrode models reviewed.

row.entry: Particle packing based on CT imaging is used to model the electrode in Batts3D
Matlab based finite differences scheme used to calculate tortuosity directly from 3D image data
Pseudo-2-dimensional model using a combination of porous electrode theory and the concentrated solution theory
Wolfram Mathematica based tool that fits ellipsoids to 2D image data to estimate the Bruggeman exponent
Pore network model used to calculate transport properties from 3D image data
A 3 phase homogenisation method is used on 3D image data
COMSOL based multi-scale model based on 3D image data, utilises representative elementary volume to reduce computation time
A mathematical model for the homogenisation of porous insertion electrodes based on non-equilibrium thermodynamics
COMSOL based model to examine effects of diffusion induced stresses in the electrodes
A mathematical model for the homogenisation of a 2 phase porous electrode is presented
Star CCM+ model used to calculate tortuosity from 3D image data
An analytical framework to extend the Bruggeman relation to incorporate the effects of mesoscale tortuosity
C＋＋ code used to populate domain with particles based on 2D image data, then used to simulate galvanostatic discharge
Extends the previous model to include heat generation

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 4th Annual CDT Conference in Energy Storage and Its Applications, Professor Andrew Cruden, 2019, 07–19, University of Southampton, U.K.

head.ce:title: Physics based modelling of porous lithium ion battery electrodes—A review

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Technology Research Group, Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences, University of Southampton, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Energy Technology Research Group, Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences, University of Southampton

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Technology Research Group, Faculty of Engineering and Physical Sciences, University of Southampton, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mathematical models have been used extensively to simulate physical and electrochemical processes occurring inside lithium-ion batteries. Physical based models, coupled with experimental validation, have revealed greater scientific understanding of the processes inside the battery. A region of specific interest is the porous electrode. However, the heterogeneous geometry of the porous structure presents practical difficulties in developing suitable models. The present paper is a review of the studies on the physical modelling of lithium ion porous electrodes. Here we review common methods to model the (de)intercalation behaviour of porous Li-ion battery electrodes. Advantages and drawbacks are contrasted to highlight some challenges that suggest directions and priorities for further research in the field.

ce:def-description.ce:para:
Intrinsic diffusion coefficient for Li+, m
2
/s

Effective diffusion coefficient for Li+, m
2
/s

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have emerged as the favoured on-board vehicle energy store due to their comparatively high power and energy densities in comparison to other rechargeable batteries [1]. Li-ion batteries show long cycle life, good electrochemical and thermal stability, low self-discharge rate and rapid charging–discharging capability [2]. They have also been used extensively within the consumer electronics industry for this reason [3]. More recently, Li-ion batteries are being installed in large stationary applications in order to support the higher penetration of renewables onto the grid. An example of this is the National Grid 200 MW enhanced frequency response tender [4].

Mathematical models have been used to simulate the physical and electrochemical processes occurring inside batteries in order to aid understanding and design [5]. A variety of length scales can be modelled, ranging from the atomistic (density functional theory) to pack level. Larger length scale models generally use empirical methods in order to predict the operation of the battery. However, empirical models have domain-specific strengths that limit utility outside the envisioned application. Physical models, models based on fundamental physical and chemical equations [6], provide a way of understanding the processes occurring within the battery but generally require a greater amount of computing power compared to empirical models. A region of specific interest is the porous electrode, where lithium de/intercalation is governed. However, the complex geometry of the porous structure has resulted in practical difficulties in developing a suitable model.

Doyle and Newman developed one of the most popular Li-ion battery models (commonly referred to as the Newman model) based on the porous electrode and concentrated solution theory [7,8]. This model used work from 1975 by Newman and Tiedemann who developed the macroscopic porous electrode theory for battery applications [6,9]. The pseudo-2D (P2D) model assumes that the porous electrode is made of equally sized, isotropic, homogeneous spherical particles [7]. This homogeneous description of electrode microstructure results in smooth, uniform intercalation/de-intercalation of lithium inside the electrode and has proven to be successful in characterising discharge/charge behaviours particularly at low to moderate rates [10]. In reality, however, Li-ion electrodes are very heterogeneous structures [11].

The P2D model has been shown to fail to predict phenomena related to inhomogeneous structure of the electrode microstructure such as performance drop at high rates [12]. The Bruggeman equations correlate tortuosity factors of porous media with their porosity and are commonly used in modelling Li-ion transport through a flooded porous electrode. This correlation is based closely on previous work by Lorentz [13] and Lorenz [14] and has found usage in fields such as optics, electromagnetics and electrochemical research [15]. The Bruggeman relation is found to only be valid in situations where the insulating phase is present in a low volume fraction and represented by random, isotropic spheres. It cannot account for directional differences which arise from non-spherical particle packing. Applying this correlation to heterogeneous structures (e.g. battery electrodes) is invalid [16] and more complex approaches are required to sufficiently characterise the transport properties of the material and its resulting tortuosity factor [15]. This situation is further explained in [17] and [18], where it is stated that path length analysis can only be used to quantify tortuosity factor in simple capillary type geometries, it cannot be used reliably in more complex pore networks [19]. Therefore, there is a need for a model that utilises real microstructure as a basis for the mathematical model.

The tortuosity factor, τ , is defined as:
(1)
τ = ε
D
D
e f f
where ε is the volume fraction of the conducting phase, D is the intrinsic diffusivity of the conducting phase and
D
eff
is the effective diffusivity through the porous volume where the second phase is insulating [20].

Recent advances in X-ray computed tomography (XCT) have made nano-scale 3D microstructure imaging attractive to experimentally characterise battery electrodes. Nano-XCT offers the capability to capture the 3D structure of porous electrodes non-destructively by reconstructing a 3D image from a stack of 2D slices. Other models have been developed that use computationally generated geometry [21,22]; however, by imaging the electrode directly, the microstructure is suitable without a priori knowledge. Imaging of Li-ion battery electrodes is carried out for two reasons, first to characterise the 3D microstructure, particle distribution and perform pore scale morphological and transport property analysis [23–26]. The second reason is to directly utilise the reconstructed 3D microstructure to simulate multi-physics phenomena occurring inside the cell, such as discharge/charge performance [27] and thermal behaviour [28].

TauFactor is an open source MatLab application for efficiently calculating the tortuosity factor, volume fraction, surface area and triple phase boundary density from image based microstructural data. This tool provides a computational platform for medium sized datasets (108 voxels) [29]. TauFactor utilises a ghost node approach in order to determine the Dirichlet boundary conditions for edge nodes. It also employs an over relaxation iterative Jacobi method in order to improve computational time. Furthermore, the voxel volume array is checkerboarded to decompose it into vectorised subdomains for the conducting phase. A direct solution method utilising matrix inversion takes over 2 h to solve a 106 voxel volume, whilst the TauFactor method solves an equivalent volume in less than 30 s [29]. However, the application is not easily parallelisable and can only be run on shared memory machines, this means that with large datasets (above 108 voxels), calculations begin to take hours and days.

The software takes an input file where the domain has been thresholded into solid and fluid phases in a per voxel basis. From this it calculates the tortuosity factor as well as a more detailed report: percolation fraction, a graph of volume fraction variation, a map of total flux through each voxel can all be generated. The flux map, in the case of current flow, could help in understanding local degradation mechanisms through overheating. This calculation is performed directly on the thresholded domain, as opposed to re-meshing the microstructure as is usual for FEM-based approaches. Four different representative element volume analyses can also be implemented in order to directly determine what volume needs to be further worked on [29].

As the model uses an assumption of perfect tomography, where voxels containing > 50% of a phase are modelled as a voxel entirely of that phase, true surface area and triple phase boundary densities are subject to significant error. Correction factors can be applied but it is key to be aware of this limitation. Fig. 1 is a graph of 8 different tortuosity factor calculation methods and it shows that there is substantial variation across the various methods. A significant result from this graph is that the Bruggeman correlation is shown to consistently underestimate the value of tortuosity when compared to the other methods. TauFactor provides a way of standardising the calculation of tortuosity factor by working from the original steady state diffusive flow definition [29].

Richardson et al. [31] developed a way to determine effective transport coefficients of composite materials through a mathematical homogenisation approach. A similar homogenisation approach has also used by Fellner and Kovtunenko [32], Landstorfer [33] and Gully et al. [34]. Effective transport properties are defined such that an equivalent homogeneous material would carry the same flux as the original composite material using the same imposed boundary conditions [35]. Namely, the effective transport coefficients represent a method to replace a complex heterogeneous structure with a simple homogeneous material that has the same macroscale properties, as seen in Fig. 2. The study in [34] considers three distinct phases within a Li-ion battery cathode: the electrolyte
Ω
E
, the active particles
Ω
AP
and the carbon doped binder
Ω
B
. It also states that recent studies have incorporated the concept of tortuosity factor in order to be able to understand transport properties of electrodes. However, it goes on to state that calculating tortuosity factor is not possible directly even if the entire microstructure is known, and it can only be found by first calculating the effective transport properties of the microstructure and then performing a reverse calculation. Indeed this is the method discussed by Cooper et al. where steady state diffusive flow is calculated across the heterogeneous structure in order to calculate the diffusivity coefficient, which is then used to calculate the tortuosity factor [29]. Gully et al. [34] state that if the original objective is to calculate the effective transport properties then this leads to a circular argument. Tortuosity factor is a convenient way to characterise the flow path resistance but ultimately it is the effective transport coefficients that define the equations.

Homogenisation is achieved through using analytic continuation methods which deal with geometrical information separately from material properties, in order to calculate effective transport properties. This method also gives rigorous maxima and minima bounds which allow comparison of how a given microstructure performs in comparison to a geometrically optimal configuration. A number of assumptions are made in order to use this method, namely:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Ω
0
is periodic in all three directions.

Ω
0
is a union of three subdomains
Ω
AP
,
Ω
B
and
Ω
E
corresponding to three distinct phases, i.e.
Ω
0
=
Ω
AP
∪
Ω
B
∪
Ω
E
.

Each of the phases
Ω
AP
,
Ω
B
and
Ω
E
, are isotropic.

Each phase is characterised by non-zero conductivity coefficients
σ
AP
,
σ
B
and
σ
E
.

ce:section.ce:para: Focused ion beam-scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM) is used to capture microstructural electrode data, this is then post-processed in order to achieve three phase segmented datasets that can be used to solve the homogenisation tensor. The final datasets are downsampled in order to reduce the processing power required to solve the resultant computational problem and consist of a 51 × 51 × 51 set of voxels. It is found that the homogenised microstructures yield different effective transport coefficients to the constant value predicted by the Bruggeman formula. It is also found that post-processing filters applied to microstructural dataset do not change the transport coefficients significantly. Lastly, it is found that for the specific sample chosen, the diffusion coefficients vary by a standard deviation of 6% depending on the direction of flow chosen. The downsampling performed to reduce the computational load is considered the main inaccuracy of the proposed approach and the authors suggest building a software tool that can make use of parallelisation to solve the problem on a suitably large supercomputer [34].

Pore network modelling is a method to simulate transport inside porous materials [36]. As opposed to solving nth-order partial differential equations, the heterogeneous porous electrode is approximated as a network of pipes. The network consists of varying size and connectivity pores and throats, that are chosen to match the known physical structure, obtained either by imaging techniques [37] or computer generated structures [38]. The transport physics is solved using a 1D finite differences scheme. This scheme allows the relationship between structure and flow properties to be characterised [39].

OpenPNM is an opensource [40] software package developed in Python for pore network simulations. Unlike traditionally used continuum models, OpenPNM does not require constitutive relationships for multiphase transport properties, such as effective diffusivity, meaning transport properties can be predicted as a function of the porous structure. Secondly, treating the porous medium as a volume-averaged continuum results in pore-scale phenomena remaining unresolved, as only the average amount of a fluid phase is known in each computational node. One of the most powerful uses of this model is to simulate transport phenomena through the porous electrode. This allows choke points within the 3D space to be recognised and, through experimental iteration, could be used to tailor electrode design to improve power capabilities.

Kashkooli et al. [41] developed a multiscale platform in order to model Li-ion battery electrodes based on real microstructure morphology. This multiscale platform consists of the microscale level where the morphology of the electrode is modelled and the macroscale level where discharge/charge is simulated. The microscale domain is a representative elementary volume of the total reconstructed electrode; the macroscale is a 1 dimensional sandwich model of Li foil – separator – cathode to simulate a half-cell reaction. These two scales are coupled in real time, meaning the calculated intercalation flux from the macroscale is used as a boundary condition for the microscale.

In the macroscale, governing equations of mass and charge transfer are developed for each component of the 1D cell structure, including: lithium iron phosphate (LFP) porous cathode, porous and ion permeable membrane separator, Li foil counter electrode, and the electrolyte. The model used to simulate transport of lithium ions inside the electrolyte is the concentrated solution theory. The lithium ion intercalation is estimated from the Butler–Volmer​ equation. In the microscale, conservation of mass is governed by Fick’s mass transfer equation [41].

This model utilised a finite element type simulation, by taking a representative elemental volume (REV) of the electrode, in this case 750 nm × 750 nm × 750 nm. The calculations were then performed using COMSOL commercial software. This model was found to perform more accurately than homogeneous models (e.g. Newman Pseudo 2D); however, it does not take into account bulk heterogeneities due to its use of REV. This method requires re-meshing the electrode dataset into polyhedral elements to be used in the FEA simulation, however, it does not require downsampling as was used by the homogenisation method.

In [42], Shearing et al. compare three different X-ray resolutions: 597 nm voxel size, 65 nm voxel size and 15 nm voxel size using commercial CFD software. The two coarser resolutions are found to have excellent agreement in both solid/pore fractions and tortuosity. There is a slight increase in porosity which is believed to be due to the improved resolution revealing smaller pore geometries. The finest resolution reveals significantly higher values for porosity, this is believed to be due to this resolution revealing the internal pore space of the particles. The two coarser resolutions find pore connectivity of near to 100% suggesting that the electrolyte phase is very well percolated. However, the most noticeable difference between these two resolutions is the volume specific surface area which is more than twice as large, this is due to the increased resolution [42].

For bulk porosity and tortuosity measurements, these lower resolutions can be tolerated with little loss to data quality whilst also increasing analysis volumes, leading to statistical advantages. Surface calculations are, however, very sensitive to length scale. The highest resolution X-ray has a large discrepancy in porosity. This is as the sample is so small that the entire volume is within a single particle. The porosity of this image, therefore, represents internal defects in the solid structure. No connection between these internal pores and the bulk pores is witnessed, meaning there is no penetration of electrolyte into the solid structure, so 4.5% of the volume is chemically inactive [42]. These samples are then imported into commercial CFD software, Star CCM+, in order to calculate tortuosity based on Fick’s and Fourier’s Laws using adaptive polyhedral volume meshes. It is found that tortuosity varies in all directions which is in agreement with studies of fuel cells [42].

batts3D is a set of python-based software libraries written to model the electrochemical response of heterogeneous rechargeable porous electrodes in three dimensions based on the discrete element method. Vijayaraghavan et al. [43] used batts3D to extend the widely used Bruggeman relation to incorporate the effects of mesoscale tortuosity. This method could then be used to combine a mixture of two porous particle systems of differing length scales, into a single macroscopic tortuosity expression that agreed with experimental results. Chung et al. [44] studied the electrochemical and chemo-mechanical response of LiMn
2
O
4
(LMO) cathodes based on the XCT method. Both reconstructed images and computationally generated data were simulated using batts3D. Their simulations show that particle size polydispersity of microstructures impacts the local chemical and electrical behaviour of a porous electrode.

Yan et al. [27] simulated the discharge behaviour of LiCoO2 (LCO) based on nano-XCT data. They developed an in-house model using C＋＋ to build a microstructure based on 2D scanned images. Their results show that the distributions of electrolyte concentration, current density, over potential and intercalation reaction rate are significantly different from the results obtained from the P2D model. Furthermore, the microstructure inhomogeneity is found to be responsible for the performance loss, particularly at high discharge rates. Yan et al. [28] then extended this model to simulate the heat generation during galvanostatic discharge in LCO microstructure. Their results show that the simulation based on reconstructed microstructure predicts more heat generation than the P2D model at high discharge rates. The simulation based on the reconstructed microstructure includes the heterogeneity of the structure, distributing the physical and electrochemical properties throughout the electrode. The authors attributed the higher predicted heat generation to this wider electrochemical properties distribution.

Similar methods can be used to compute further properties within the electrode. Lim et al. [25] modelled diffusion-induced stress inside LCO particles which were reconstructed using XCT. Stress–strain relations with a lithium ion concentration term and a diffusion equation with hydrostatic stress effect were used in order to simulate these effects. Their results demonstrated that the highest von Mises and Tresca stresses in reconstructed particles are several times greater than those obtained from a simple spherical or ellipsoid particle with the same volume and that the intercalation stresses of the particles are dependent on their geometric characteristics.

A summary of the models reviewed can be seen in Table 1. Recent years have seen the introduction of imaged electrode datasets that introduce heterogeneity to the model domains, increasing accuracy [40,41]. However, considering the models presented, there are still a number of open issues that should be addressed in future studies.

Across the image-driven simulations, a variety of imaging techniques and standards are employed. XCT and FIB-SEM both operate at varying resolutions but both obtain 3D image data. Whilst SEM and TEM both produce 2D image data. Shearing et al. [42] started to investigate the effect of image resolution on the results of simulation, however, more rigorous study is required to fully characterise the effects. Imaging the same electrode sample at a number of resolutions, at the same point in space and with the same overall dimensions, would allow a direct comparison of the effect of resolution on structural properties key to solving transport equations, such as tortuosity and porosity.

One of the key themes from these models is the limitation of computational power. Representative elementary volumes, downsampling, particle approximations and pore network modelling are all used to try to reduce the computational resource required. Each of these comes with an associated limitation. REVs reduce the volume of electrode being modelled. Macroscale heterogeneity, manufacturing defects [45] and structured electrodes [46,47] could all be missed by using this method. Correlation of REV size against transport properties has been performed but further investigation is required in this field. Downsampling and particle approximations reduce the microscale interactions of the particles, and changes the contact surface area between the three phases. Finally, pore network modelling sacrifices some of the mathematical rigour by simplifying the interactions taking place. Being able to fully model the electrochemical interactions at the resolution obtained by the imaging method would require a large amount of computer power. However, a suitably parallelisable code written specifically for high performance computing would allow such calculations to be performed, revealing further scientific insight into the performance of lithium ion batteries.

Implementing one of the models discussed can take a large amount of time. One of the steps that is commonly carried out in these studies is the re-meshing of the image data. FEA studies require the cube shaped voxels to be re-meshed into polyhedral shapes, pore network modelling requires the network to be created from the image data and the homogenisation requires the image data to be coarsened. Each of these steps requires human oversight and is hard to automate, and could lead to additional approximation errors whilst re-meshing the dataset. However, as shown by Cooper et al. [29], it is possible to carry out calculations directly on the imaged dataset. By solving the physical equations directly on the voxels, a step is removed from the simulation process, improving both speed and accuracy of the modelling process.

This study has reviewed the open literature in physics based lithium ion porous electrode modelling. Physical based models, coupled with experimental validation, have helped reveal greater scientific understanding of the processes inside the battery. The porous electrode is of specific interest as it is where lithium de/intercalation occurs. However, the complex geometry of the porous structure has resulted in practical difficulties, due to the size of datasets involved, in developing a suitable model. The models developed to approach this problem have been presented in this paper.

It is found that there has been a general trend to improve accuracy of physical based models by including the heterogeneity of the porous electrode structure. This is contrast to the traditional method of assuming the electrode to be a homogeneous structure through using the Bruggeman relation. Both image obtained and computationally generated datasets have been used to include this complexity in the model domain.

Considering the models that have been presented there are still a number of open questions that remain to be solved: (1) to fully characterise the effects that image resolution has on transport properties, such as porosity and tortuosity; (2) to develop an easily parallelisable code written specifically for high performance computing, allowing larger image datasets to be solved; (3) to remove the re-meshing step from the simulation process, improving speed and accuracy of the whole modelling workflow.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the EPSRC Centre for Doctoral Training (CDT), United Kingdom in Energy Storage and its Applications [grant ref: EP/R021295/1]. The authors would also like to thank Ewan Fraser for his enlightening discussion and useful comments on this topic.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable options for electric vehicle technologies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Poullikkas A. (2015) Sustainable options for electric vehicle technologies. Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 41 1277-1287.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of commercially available lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Johnson B. A. & R. E. White (1998) Characterization of commercially available lithium-ion batteries. Journal of power sources, 70 48-54.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery cost analysis in PV-household application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Naumann M., R. C. Karl, C. N. Truong, A. Jossen & H. C. Hesse (2015) Lithium-ion battery cost analysis in PV-household application. Energy Procedia, 73 37-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Frequency response services designed for energy storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Greenwood D., K. Y. Lim, C. Patsios, P. Lyons, Y. S. Lim & P. Taylor (2017) Frequency response services designed for energy storage. Applied Energy, 203 115-127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiscale modelling and numerical simulation of rechargeable lithium ion batteries: concepts, methods and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Franco A. A. (2013) Multiscale modelling and numerical simulation of rechargeable lithium ion batteries: concepts, methods and challenges. Rsc Advances, 3 13027-13058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of simplified Pseudo-two-Dimensional models of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jokar A., B. Rajabloo, M. Désilets & M. Lacroix (2016) Review of simplified Pseudo-two-Dimensional models of lithium-ion batteries. Journal of Power Sources, 327 44-55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The use of mathematical modeling in the design of lithium/polymer battery systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Doyle M. & J. Newman (1995) The use of mathematical modeling in the design of lithium/polymer battery systems. Electrochimica Acta, 40 2191-2196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simulation and optimization of the dual lithium ion insertion cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fuller T. F., M. Doyle & J. Newman (1994) Simulation and optimization of the dual lithium ion insertion cell. Journal of the Electrochemical Society, 141 1-10.
Newman J. & W. Tiedemann (1975) Porous-electrode theory with battery applications. AIChE Journal, 21 25-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of electrode physical and chemical properties on lithium-ion battery performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chabot V., S. Farhad, Z. Chen, A. S. Fung, A. Yu & F. Hamdullahpur (2013) Effect of electrode physical and chemical properties on lithium-ion battery performance. International Journal of Energy Research, 37 1723-1736.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of the 3-dimensional microstructure of a graphite negative electrode from a Li-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shearing P. R., L. E. Howard, P. S. Jørgensen, N. P. Brandon & S. J. Harris (2010) Characterization of the 3-dimensional microstructure of a graphite negative electrode from a Li-ion battery. Electrochemistry communications, 12 374-377.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective transport properties of LiMn2O4 electrode via particle-scale modeling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gupta A., J. H. Seo, X. Zhang, W. Du, A. M. Sastry & W. Shyy (2011) Effective transport properties of LiMn2O4 electrode via particle-scale modeling. Journal of The Electrochemical Society, 158 A487-A497.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ueber die Beziehung zwischen der Fortpflanzungsgeschwindigkeit des Lichtes und der Körperdichte

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lorentz H. A. (1880) Ueber die Beziehung zwischen der Fortpflanzungsgeschwindigkeit des Lichtes und der Körperdichte. Annalen der Physik, 245 641-665.
Lorenz L. (1880) Ueber die refractionsconstante. Annalen der Physik, 247 70-103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the origin and application of the Bruggeman correlation for analysing transport phenomena in electrochemical systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tjaden B., S. J. Cooper, D. J. Brett, D. Kramer & P. R. Shearing (2016) On the origin and application of the Bruggeman correlation for analysing transport phenomena in electrochemical systems. Current Opinion in Chemical Engineering, 12 44-51.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validity of the Bruggeman relation for porous electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chung D.-W., M. Ebner, D. R. Ely, V. Wood & R. E. García (2013) Validity of the Bruggeman relation for porous electrodes. Modelling and Simulation in Materials Science and Engineering, 21 074009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Geometrical characterization of interconnected phase networks in three dimensions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jørgensen P. S., K. V. Hansen, R. Larsen & J. R. Bowen (2011) Geometrical characterization of interconnected phase networks in three dimensions. Journal of microscopy, 244 45-58.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tortuosity characterization of 3D microstructure at nano-scale for energy storage and conversion materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen-Wiegart Y.-c. K., R. DeMike, C. Erdonmez, K. Thornton, S. A. Barnett & J. Wang (2014) Tortuosity characterization of 3D microstructure at nano-scale for energy storage and conversion materials. Journal of Power Sources, 249 349-356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On tortuosity and the tortuosity factor in flow and diffusion through porous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Epstein N. (1989) On tortuosity and the tortuosity factor in flow and diffusion through porous media. Chemical engineering science, 44 777-779.
